content:
Using **cross-validation to mitigate overfitting during model choice** grew alongside **automated hyperparameter search** in applied statistics and machine learning. Early practitioners tuned on a **single validation slice**; as grids widened, **selection bias**—picking settings that fit that slice's noise—became obvious.

**Resampling for assessment** (Stone, 1970s) provided rotating validation folds. ML workflows adopted **k-fold inner loops** inside **grid search** so penalty strengths and tree depths were chosen by **mean fold performance**, not one partition.

**Varma & Simon (2006)** formalized **nested cross-validation** to address **tuning optimism**: inner CV selects, outer CV scores. Libraries such as **scikit-learn** (`GridSearchCV`, `RandomizedSearchCV`, `cross_val_score` with nested estimators) made nested tuning accessible—while the interpretive rule remains: **inner scores rank; outer scores (or a locked test set) report**.

Today, mitigation-focused CV sits beside **regularization** and **early stopping** in the overfitting toolkit: CV improves **which** model you ship; other tools change **how** it is trained.

timeline.label: Hold-out tuning limits (1990s–2000s)
timeline.description: Growing hyperparameter grids on fixed validation slices produce optimistic winners—motivation for CV-based selection.

timeline.label: Grid search with inner CV
timeline.description: ML libraries embed k-fold loops inside hyperparameter search so mean fold metrics drive penalty and depth choices.

timeline.label: Nested CV formalized
timeline.description: Varma & Simon separate inner selection from outer estimation to mitigate tuning overfitting.

timeline.label: Integrated sklearn workflows
timeline.description: GridSearchCV, Pipeline, and cross_val_score compose leakage-safe inner tuning and optional nested outer scoring.
